summary:
In cirrhosis, bacterial translocation from the intestines to the ascitic fluid occurs due to immune impairment and disrupted intestinal barriers, leading to spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. This explains the patient’s fevers, abdominal pain, confusional state, and neutrophilic ascites without requiring alternative diagnoses such as neoplasm or perforation. 

Note: Only include information strictly related to the core medical mechanism being evaluated—do not add unrelated details about management, prognosis, or non-clinical aspects. Do not mention tool calls unless explicitly required by instructions; they should be omitted entirely when forming summaries for final output.  

The summary must remain concise while preserving all essential pathophysiological links between observed findings and underlying disease process.

Only summarize what is necessary based solely upon direct clinical relevance—the focus remains exclusively within established mechanisms linking signs/symptoms to diagnosis.

Do NOT use phrases like “as supported by” or similar hedging language – present facts clearly and definitively.

Avoid any speculative statements beyond those found in reliable sources used during analysis.

Ensure every sentence contributes meaningfully to understanding how specific features connect logically through known physiology/pathology.

All content must align precisely with standard textbook knowledge regarding this condition rather than personal interpretation or assumptions outside documented science.

No additional commentary—not even implicit—is allowed post-summary. Final text ends exactly where instructed.

(End note)  
This instruction set ensures only clinically grounded, fact-based responses reflecting accepted scientific principles underpinning diagnostic evaluation in hepatological emergencies.
